revision.text:
[[af:Amitabha]][[ar:أميتابها]][[az:Amitabha]][[bg:Амитабха]][[bn:অমিতাভ বুদ্ধ]][[bo:འོད་དཔག་མེད།]][[bxr:Абида]][[ca:Amitabha]][[cdo:Ŏ̤-mì-tò̤-hŭk]][[cs:Amitábha]][[de:Amitabha]][[el:Αμιτάμπα]][[en:Amitābha]][[eo:Budho Amitabo]][[es:Amitābha]][[et:Amitābha]][[eu:Amitābha]][[fa:آمیتابه]][[fi:Amitabha]][[fr:Amitābha]][[gan:幫助:阿彌陀佛]][[he:אמיטאבהה]][[hi:अमिताभ बुद्ध]][[hu:Amitábha]][[id:Amitābha]][[it:Amitabha Buddha]][[ja:阿弥陀如来]][[jv:Amitabha]][[ka:ამიტაბჰა]][[kk:Амитабха]][[km:អមិតាភ]][[ko:아미타불]][[lt:Amitabha]][[lv:Amitābha]][[ml:അമിതാഭൻ]][[mn:Аюуш бурхан]][[mr:अमिताभ]][[ms:Amitabha]][[my:အမိတာဘ]][[nl:Amitabha]][[no:Amitābha]][[pl:Amitabha]][[pt:Amitaba]][[ro:Amitābha]][[ru:Амитабха]][[sh:Amitabha]][[simple:Amitabha]][[si:අමිතාභ බුදුන් වහන්සේ]][[sq:Amitaba Buda]][[sv:Amitabha]][[ta:அமிதாப புத்தர்]][[te:అమితాభ బుద్ధుడు]][[th:พระอมิตาภพุทธะ]][[tl:Amitābha]][[tr:Amitabha]][[uk:Амітабга]][[uz:Amitabxa]][[vi:A-di-đà]][[wuu:阿弥陀佛]][[zh-min-nan:O-bí-tô-hu̍t]][[zh-yue:阿彌陀佛]][[zh:阿弥陀佛]] {{short description|Celestial Buddha of Buddhism|qq=}} {{about|the Buddha||Amitabha (disambiguation)|qq=}} {{Infobox Buddha | name = Amitābha | image = Seated Amida Nyorai (Amitabha), Kamakura period, 12th-13th century, wood with gold leaf and inlaid crystal eyes - Tokyo National Museum - DSC05345.JPG | caption = Amitābha statue in gold leaf with inlaid crystal eyes. {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|qid=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, Japan | sanskrit_name =
{{plainlist| * {{lang|sa|अमिताभः|qq=}} * {{ill|International Alphabet of Sanskrit Transliteration|en|International Alphabet of Sanskrit Transliteration|qid=Q194486|lt=IAST|1=International Alphabet of Sanskrit Transliteration}}: Amitābhaḥ }}
{{plainlist| * {{lang|sa|अमितायुः|qq=}} * IAST: Amitāyuḥ }} | sinhala_name = | bengali_name = {{plainlist| * {{lang|bn|অমিতাভ|qq=}} * {{transl|bn|Amitābha|qq=}} }} | pali_name = | burmese_name = | chinese_name = {{plainlist| * 阿弥陀佛, 阿彌陀佛 * {{ill|Pinyin|en|Pinyin|qid=Q42222|lt=Pinyin|1=Pinyin}}: Ēmítuó fó{{cite web|title=阿彌陀佛 | date= 25 June 2023| url= https://en.m.wiktionary.org/wiki/阿彌陀佛#Chinese|qq=}} }} | japanese_name = {{ruby-ja|阿弥陀仏|あみだぶつ|qq=}}, {{ruby-ja|阿弥陀如来|あみだにょらい|qq=}}
([[:en:Romaji|Romaji]]: Amida Butsu, Amida Nyorai) }} '''Amitābha'''Lévi, Sylvain; Takakusu, Junjir; Demiéville, Paul; Watanabe, Kaigyoku (1929). [https://archive.org/details/hbgirindictionna0103lvuoft Hobogirin]: Dictionnaire encyclopédique de bouddhisme d'après les sources chinoises et japonaises, Paris: Maisonneuve, vols. 1–3, pp. 24–29 ({{IPA-sa|ɐmɪˈtaːbʱɐ|sa|qq=}}) is the principal {{ill|The Buddha|en|The Buddha|qid=Q9441|lt=The Buddha|1=The Buddha}} of {{ill|Pure Land Buddhism|en|Pure Land Buddhism|qid=Q262244|lt=Pure Land Buddhism|1=Pure Land Buddhism}}. He is also known as '''Amitāyus''', which is understood to be his enjoyment body (''{{ill|Saṃbhogakāya|en|Saṃbhogakāya|qid=Q2608727|lt=Saṃbhogakāya|1=Saṃbhogakāya}}'').{{Cite web |title=Buddha Amitabha and Amitayus: The Distinctive Differences in Iconography |url=https://enlightenmentthangka.com/blogs/thangka/bu11ha-amitabha-amitayus |access-date=2023-08-24 |website=Enlightenment Thangka |language=en|qq=}} In {{ill|Vajrayana|en|Vajrayana|qid=Q172175|lt=Vajrayana Buddhism|1=Vajrayana}}, Amitābha is known for his longevity, {{ill|Vijñāna|en|Vijñāna|qid=Q628565|lt=discernment|1=Vijñāna}}, {{ill|Saṃjñā|en|Saṃjñā|qid=Q2558517|lt=pure perception|1=Saṃjñā}}, and the purification of {{ill|Skandha|en|Skandha|qid=Q747858|lt=aggregates|1=Skandha}} with deep awareness of the emptiness of all phenomena. Amitābha is associated with the {{ill|Diamond Realm|en|Diamond Realm|qid=Q518314|lt=Diamond Realm|1=Diamond Realm}} (''vajradhātu''), whereas Amitāyus is associated with the {{ill|Womb Realm|en|Womb Realm|qid=Q855739|lt=Womb Realm|1=Womb Realm}} (''garbhakoṣadhātu'').Charles Muller, "Buddha of Immeasurable Life 無量壽佛" Digital Dictionary of Buddhism, http://www.buddhism-dict.net/cgi-bin/xpr-11b.pl?71.xml+id(%27b7121-91cf-58fd-4f5b%27). == Doctrine == [[File:Ming era statue of Amitabha (阿彌陀佛 or 阿弥陀佛; Āmítuófó), one of the Five Tathagathas (五方佛 Wǔfāngfó) or Five Wisdom Buddhas (五智如来 Wǔzhì Rúlái) at Huayan Temple (华严寺), Shanxi, China.jpg|left|thumb|{{ill|Ming dynasty|en|Ming dynasty|qid=Q9903|lt=Ming dynasty|1=Ming dynasty}} (1368–1644) statue of Amitābha in {{ill|Huayan Temple (Datong)|en|Huayan Temple (Datong)|qid=Q15932853|lt=Huayan Temple|1=Huayan Temple (Datong)}} in {{ill|Datong|en|Datong|qid=Q72877|lt=Datong|1=Datong}}, {{ill|Shanxi|en|Shanxi|qid=Q46913|lt=Shanxi|1=Shanxi}}, China]] [[File:Buddha Amithaba.jpg|thumb|left|Buddha Amitābha in {{ill|Tibetan Buddhism|en|Tibetan Buddhism|qid=Q483889|lt=Tibetan Buddhism|1=Tibetan Buddhism}}, traditional {{ill|Thangka|en|Thangka|qid=Q916651|lt=Thangka|1=Thangka}} painting]] [[File:Kamakura Bu11a Daibutsu front 1885.jpg|thumb|The Great Buddha of Kamakura in the {{ill|Kōtoku-in|en|Kōtoku-in|qid=Q672056|lt=Kōtoku-in|1=Kōtoku-in}} temple]] [[File:Gilt-bronze Amitābha at the Geungnakjeon Hall of Bulguksa Temple, Korea.jpg|thumb|Gilt-bronze statue of Amithabha from {{ill|Unified Silla|en|Unified Silla|qid=Q715257|lt=8th century Silla|1=Unified Silla}}, {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|qid=Q18097|lt=Korea|1=Korea}}. Located at [[:en:Bulguk-sa|Bulguk-sa]] temple.]] [[File:Amitabha with Eight Great Bodhisattvas (Tokugawa Art Museum) 2.jpg|thumb|Korean Amitabha with Eight Great Bodhisattvas (Tokugawa Art Museum)]] [[File:Museum of Ethnology Vienna 001 retouched.jpg|thumb|Statue of the Buddha Amitāyus (Mongolia, 18th century)]] ===Attainment of Buddhahood=== According to the ''[[:en:Infinite Life Sutra|Larger Sūtra of Immeasurable Life]]'', Amitābha was, in very ancient times and possibly in another system of worlds, a monk named Dharmākara. In some versions of the {{ill|Sutra|en|Sutra|qid=Q209680|lt=sūtra|1=Sutra}}, Dharmākara is described as a former king who, having come into contact with Buddhist teachings through the bu11ha {{ill|en:Lokeśvararāja|en|en:Lokeśvararāja|qid=Q6668652|lt=en:Lokeśvararāja|1=Lokeśvararāja}}, renounced his throne. He then resolved to become a Buddha and to create a ''{{IAST|[[bu11hakṣetra]]|qq=}}'' (literally "bu11ha-field", often called a "Pureland" or "Buddha Land": a realm existing in the primordial universe outside of ordinary reality, produced by a bu11ha's merit) possessed of many perfections. These resolutions were expressed in his [[s:Amitabha's forty-eight vows|forty-eight vows]], which set out the type of Pureland Dharmākara aspired to create, the conditions under which beings might be born into that world, and what kind of beings they would be when {{ill|Rebirth (Buddhism)|en|Rebirth (Buddhism)|qid=Q1480392|lt=reborn|1=Rebirth (Buddhism)}} there. In the versions of the sutra widely known in China, Vietnam, Korea and Japan, Dharmākara's eighteenth vow was that any being in any universe desiring to be reborn into Amitābha's [[:en:Pure land|Pure land]] ({{CJKV|c=淨土|p=jìngtǔ|r=jōdo|k=정토|rr=jeongto|v=tịnh độ|s=|t=|qq=}}) and calling upon his name with sincerity, even as few as ten times will be guaranteed rebirth there. His nineteenth vow promises that he, together with his bodhisattvas and other blessed Buddhists, will appear before those who, at the moment of death, call upon him. This openness and acceptance of all kinds of people has made belief in pure lands one of the major influences in Mahāyāna Buddhism. {{ill|Pure Land Buddhism|en|Pure Land Buddhism|qid=Q262244|lt=Pure Land Buddhism|1=Pure Land Buddhism}} seems to have first become popular in {{ill|Gandhara|en|Gandhara|qid=Q213651|lt=Gandhara|1=Gandhara}}, from where it spread to {{ill|China|en|China|qid=Q148|lt=China|1=China}} infused with [[Taoism|Taoists]] and {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|qid=Q9581|lt=Confucian|1=Confucianism}} philosophy before spreading to {{ill|Central Asia|en|Central Asia|qid=Q27275|lt=Central|1=Central Asia}} and [[:en:East Asian cultural sphere|East Asia]]. The sutra goes on to explain that Amitābha, after accumulating great merit over countless lives, finally achieved bu11hahood and created a pure land called ''Sukhāvatī'' ({{ill|Sanskrit|en|Sanskrit|qid=Q11059|lt=Sanskrit|1=Sanskrit}}: "possessing happiness"). Sukhāvatī is situated in the uttermost west, beyond the bounds of our own world. By the power of his vows, Amitābha has made it possible for all who call upon him to be {{ill|Rebirth (Buddhism)|en|Rebirth (Buddhism)|qid=Q1480392|lt=reborn|1=Rebirth (Buddhism)}} into this land, there to undergo instruction by him in the {{ill|Dharma|en|Dharma|qid=Q134293|lt=Dharma|1=Dharma}} and ultimately become bodhisattvas and bu11has in their turn (the ultimate goal of Mahāyāna Buddhism). From there, these same bodhisattvas and bu11has return to our world to help yet more people while still residing in his land of {{ill|Sukhavati|en|Sukhavati|qid=Q1329565|lt=Sukhāvatī|1=Sukhavati}}, whose many virtues and joys are described. ===References in Sutras=== The earliest known reference to Amitābha in a {{ill|Buddhist texts|en|Buddhist texts|qid=Q1001051|lt=sutra|1=Buddhist texts}} is the [[Pratyutpanna Samādhi Sūtra]], translated into Chinese by {{ill|Lokaksema (Buddhist monk)|en|Lokaksema (Buddhist monk)|qid=Q370115|lt=Lokakṣema|1=Lokaksema (Buddhist monk)}} in 179 CE, with the discovery of a {{ill|Gandhari language|en|Gandhari language|qid=Q3124623|lt=Gandhari language|1=Gandhari language}} fragment of that sutra announced in 2018.{{cite journal |last1=Harrison |first1=Paul |last2=Lenz |first2=Timothy |last3=Salomon |first3=Richard |title=Fragments of a Gāndhārī Manuscript of the Pratyutpannabu11hasaṃmukhāvasthitasamādhisūtra |journal=Journal of the International Association of Buddhist Studies |date=2018 |volume=41 |pages=117–143 |doi=10.2143/JIABS.41.0.3285740|qq=}} Amitabha is mentioned in numerous Buddhist sources. For example, {{ill|Kenneth K. Tanaka|en|Kenneth K. Tanaka|qid=Q6390294|lt=Kenneth Tanaka|1=Kenneth K. Tanaka}} writes:
Thirty one Sanskrit texts and over one hundred Chinese and Tibetan translations refer to Amitabha* and/or Sukhavati*. For example, the author of the Ratnagotravibhaga sastra* (Treatise on the Buddha womb Theory) concludes his highly technical work on the tathagatagarbha* (Buddha womb or embryo) doctrine by stating: "By the merit I have acquired through [writing] this [treatise], may all living beings come to perceive the Lord Amitayus* endowed with infinite light."Tanaka, Kenneth K. 1990. ''The Dawn of Chinese Pure Land Buddhist Doctrine: Ching-ying Hui-yüanʼs Commentary on the Visualization Sutra,'' p. 12. Albany: State University of New York Press.
Jeff Wilson writes that over a fifth of the sutras in the {{ill|Taishō Tripiṭaka|en|Taishō Tripiṭaka|qid=Q837315|lt=Taishō Tripiṭaka|1=Taishō Tripiṭaka}} reference Amitābha,{{cite web |last1=Wilson |first1=Jeff |title=Pure Land Sūtras |url=https://www.oxfordbibliographies.com/display/document/obo-9780195393521/obo-9780195393521-0132.xml |website=Oxford Bibliographies Online |access-date=26 February 2024 |language=en|qq=}} but three sutras in particular have become seen as canonical in East Asian Buddhism:{{Citation|translator-last1=Inagaki |translator-first1=Hisao |title=The Three Pure Land Sutras |date=2003 |publisher=Numata Center for Buddhist Translation and Research |location=Berkeley |url=http://www.bdk.or.jp/pdf/bdk/digitaldl/dBET_ThreePureLandSutras_2003.pdf |isbn=1-886439-18-4 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140512124959/http://www.bdk.or.jp/pdf/bdk/digitaldl/dBET_ThreePureLandSutras_2003.pdf |archive-date=May 12, 2014 |qq=}} * ''[[:en:Amitayurdhyana Sutra|Amitayurdhyana Sutra]]'' * ''[[:en:Longer Sukhāvatīvyūha Sūtra|Longer Sukhāvatīvyūha Sūtra]]'' * ''[[:en:Shorter Sukhāvatīvyūha Sūtra|Shorter Sukhāvatīvyūha Sūtra]]'' Amitābha is understood as the [[bu11hahood|Buddha]] of comprehensive love. Amitābha's [[:en:Pure land|Pure land]] is described as being in the West, and he works for the enlightenment of all beings (represented iconographically as a blessing Buddha). The [[:en:Amitayurdhyana Sutra|Amitayurdhyana Sutra]] recommends and describes at length the practice of visualising Amitābha and the Pure Land. The other two sutras do not detail visualisation practices, and have been interpreted in different ways, such as the {{ill|Nianfo|en|Nianfo|qid=Q1315754|lt=Nianfo|1=Nianfo}} practice of repeatedly saying Amitābha's name. Other practices developed from these sutras include practices at the time of death, such as visualising Amitābha in the heaven (sun) over their head (Western horizon), think his name as a mantra, and leaving the body as a soul through the acupuncture point Bai Hui (百會).{{Citation needed|reason=Seems to be an esoteric Chinese practice from a specific school, where is this from?|date=February 2024|qq=}} === Nianfo === {{ill|East Asian Buddhism|en|East Asian Buddhism|qid=Q2905453|lt=East Asian Buddhist|1=East Asian Buddhism}} traditions commonly invoke Amitābha's name in a practice known as ''{{ill|Nianfo|en|Nianfo|qid=Q1315754|lt=Nianfo|1=Nianfo}}'' ({{lang|zh|念佛|qq=}}) in Chinese and ''nembutsu'' in Japanese. This is the central practice of East Asian {{ill|Pure Land Buddhism|en|Pure Land Buddhism|qid=Q262244|lt=Pure Land Buddhism|1=Pure Land Buddhism}} which is focused around Amitābha Buddha. In East Asian Pure Land traditions, the main religious practice is the recitation or chanting of the phrase 南無阿彌陀佛 ({{ill|Mandarin Chinese|en|Mandarin Chinese|qid=Q9192|lt=Mandarin|1=Mandarin Chinese}}: Nāmó Ēmítuófó, Japanese: Namu Amida Butsu) which means "Homage to Amitābha Buddha". ===Vajrayāna Buddhism=== Amitābha is also known in {{ill|Tibet|en|Tibet|qid=Q17252|lt=Tibet|1=Tibet}}, {{ill|Mongolia|en|Mongolia|qid=Q711|lt=Mongolia|1=Mongolia}}, {{ill|Nepal|en|Nepal|qid=Q837|lt=Nepal|1=Nepal}}, {{ill|India|en|India|qid=Q668|lt=India|1=India}} and other regions where {{ill|Tibetan Buddhism|en|Tibetan Buddhism|qid=Q483889|lt=Tibetan Buddhism|1=Tibetan Buddhism}} is practiced. In the {{ill|Yogatantra|en|Yogatantra|qid=Q8054352|lt=Highest Yogatantra|1=Yogatantra}} of Tibetan Buddhism, Amitābha is considered one of the {{ill|Five Tathāgatas|en|Five Tathāgatas|qid=Q265911|lt=Five Tathāgatas|1=Five Tathāgatas}} known also as the {{ill|Five Tathāgatas|en|Five Tathāgatas|qid=Q265911|lt=Five Tathāgatas|1=Five Tathāgatas}} together with [[Akshobhya]], [[Amoghasi11hi]], {{ill|Ratnasambhava|en|Ratnasambhava|qid=Q855742|lt=Ratnasambhava|1=Ratnasambhava}}, and {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|qid=Q239847|lt=Vairocana|1=Vairocana}}. Amitābha is associated with the western direction and the ''{{ill|Skandha|en|Skandha|qid=Q747858|lt=Skandha|1=Skandha}}'' of ''{{IAST|saṃjñā|qq=}}'', the aggregate of perception, or distinguishing, and the deep awareness of individuality. His co-equal is the female Buddha [[Pāṇḍaravāsinī]].Buddha Weekly, ''Five Female Buddhas or Mothers: Their Roles as Prajnas or Enlightened Wisdom — Inseparable Co-Equals Rather Than Consorts'', https://bu11haweekly.com/five-female-bu11has-or-mothers-their-roles-as-prajnas-or-enlightened-wisdom-inseparable-co-equals-rather-than-consorts/{{cite web|title=The Great Compassion Mantra – Namo Amitabha |url=http://www.geocities.com/amitabha48vows/d.htm |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090221114240/http://www.geocities.com/amitabha48vows/d.htm |archive-date=2009-02-21 |url-status=dead |qq=}}{{cite web|url=http://kaykeys.net/spirit/buddhism/bardo/4thday.html |title=Bardo: Fourth Day |publisher=Kaykeys.net |date=2005-02-07 |access-date=2012-11-07|qq=}}{{cite web|url=http://buddhism.kalachakranet.org/5_dhyani_bu11has.html |title=Symbolism of the five Dhyani Buddhas |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090308030758/http://buddhism.kalachakranet.org/5_dhyani_bu11has.html |archive-date=March 8, 2009 |qq=}}{{cite web|url=http://www.himalayanmart.com/pandara.php |title=Pandara is said to be the Prajna of Amitābha Buddha. Pandara is the same in essence with Buddha Amitābha |publisher=Himalayanmart.com |access-date=2012-11-07|qq=}}{{cite web|url=http://www.nationsonline.org/oneworld/Chinese_Customs/Guan_Yin.htm |title=Guan Yin – Bodhisattva/ Go11ess of Compassion |publisher=Nationsonline.org |date=2011-06-04 |access-date=2012-11-07|qq=}} His two main disciples (the same number as [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Gautama Buddha]]) are the {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|qid=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}}s {{ill|Vajrapani|en|Vajrapani|qid=Q1366849|lt=Vajrapani|1=Vajrapani}} and {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|en|Avalokiteśvara|qid=Q193849|lt=Avalokiteśvara|1=Avalokiteśvara}}, the former to his left and the latter to his right. In {{ill|Tibetan Buddhism|en|Tibetan Buddhism|qid=Q483889|lt=Tibetan Buddhism|1=Tibetan Buddhism}}, there exist a number of famous prayers for taking rebirth in Sukhāvatī ([[:en:Dewachen|Dewachen]]). One of these was written by {{ill|Je Tsongkhapa|en|Je Tsongkhapa|qid=Q323439|lt=Je Tsongkhapa|1=Je Tsongkhapa}}, on the request of [[Manjushri]].Georgios T. Halkias, ''Luminous Bliss: A Religious History of Pure Land Literature in Tibet Pure Land'', (''note: For a discussion and translation of the most important prayers in the Tibetan tradition see Halkias''). Amitābha is primarily invoked in Tibet during the {{ill|Phowa|en|Phowa|qid=Q2088999|lt=Phowa|1=Phowa}} practices, or invoked as Amitāyus – especially in practices relating to longevity and preventing an untimely death. The {{ill|Panchen Lama|en|Panchen Lama|qid=Q157310|lt=Panchen Lama|1=Panchen Lama}}s''Tibet is My Country: Autobiography of Thubten Jigme Norbu, Brother of the Dalai Lama as told to Heinrich Harrer'', p. 121. First published in German in 1960. English translation by Edward Fitzgerald, published 1960. Reprint, with updated new chapter, (1986): Wisdom Publications, London. {{ISBN|0-86171-045-2|qq=}}. and the {{ill|Shamarpa|en|Shamarpa|qid=Q302687|lt=Shamarpa|1=Shamarpa}}s{{cite web|url=http://www.diamondway.org/whitewater/new-teachers.html|title=Teachers: Shamar Rinpoche|access-date=2007-10-21|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071030191810/http://www.diamondway.org/whitewater/new-teachers.html|archive-date=2007-10-30|url-status=dead|qq=}} are considered to be {{ill|Emanationism|en|Emanationism|qid=Q846856|lt=emanations|1=Emanationism}} of Amitābha. In {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon Buddhism|1=Shingon Buddhism}}, Amitābha is seen as one of the {{ill|Thirteen Buddhas|en|Thirteen Buddhas|qid=Q3538361|lt=Thirteen Buddhas|1=Thirteen Buddhas}} to whom practitioners can pay homage. Shingon, like Tibetan Buddhism, also uses [[Mandala of the Two Realms|special devotional mantras]] for Amitābha, though the mantras used differ. Amitābha is also one of the Buddhas featured in the {{ill|Womb Realm|en|Womb Realm|qid=Q855739|lt=Womb Realm|1=Womb Realm}} Mandala used in {{ill|Abhisheka|en|Abhisheka|qid=Q75799|lt=Shingon practices|1=Abhisheka}}, and sits to the west, which is where the Pure Land of Amitābha is said to dwell. ==== Mantras ==== Amitābha is the center of a number of {{ill|Mantra|en|Mantra|qid=Q131510|lt=Mantra|1=Mantra}}s in {{ill|Vajrayana|en|Vajrayana|qid=Q172175|lt=Vajrayana|1=Vajrayana}} practices. The {{ill|Sanskrit|en|Sanskrit|qid=Q11059|lt=Sanskrit|1=Sanskrit}} form of the mantra of Amitābha is {{IAST|''[[:en:Oṃ|Oṃ]] amitābha hrīḥ''|qq=}}). An alternative {{ill|Tibetan Buddhism|en|Tibetan Buddhism|qid=Q483889|lt=Tibetan|1=Tibetan Buddhism}} mantra is ''Om ami dewa hri'' (Sanskrit: {{lang|sa-Latn|oṃ amideva hrīḥ|qq=}}). Amitabha's main mantra in {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon|1=Shingon Buddhism}} Buddhism is ''{{lang|sa-Latn|Om amirita teizei kara um|qq=}}'' (Japanese: {{lang|ja|オン・アミリタ・テイゼイ・カラ・ウン|qq=}}), which represents the underlying Indic form: ''{{IAST|oṃ amṛta-teje hara hūṃ|qq=}}''. == Names in various languages == [[File:longmen-hi11en-stream-temple-cave-amitabha.jpg|thumb|left|{{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|qid=Q9683|lt=Tang dynasty|1=Tang dynasty}} Amitābha sculpture, Hi11en Stream Temple Cave, {{ill|Longmen Grottoes|en|Longmen Grottoes|qid=Q273834|lt=Longmen Grottoes|1=Longmen Grottoes}}, China]] [[File:Amitabha Buddha, 1057, Museum of Fine Arts, Hanoi (2) (38468797422).jpg|thumb|Stone statue of Amitābha Buddha, {{ill|Lý dynasty|en|Lý dynasty|qid=Q878309|lt=Lý dynasty|1=Lý dynasty}}, {{ill|Phật Tích Temple|en|Phật Tích Temple|qid=Q7189921|lt=Phật Tích Temple|1=Phật Tích Temple}}, Vietnam]] [[File:Miu Fat Buddhist Monastery, The Mahavira Hall of the Ten Thousand Buddhas Hall (Hong Kong).jpg|thumb|Statues of a Buddha triad in {{ill|Miu Fat Buddhist Monastery|en|Miu Fat Buddhist Monastery|qid=Q10943052|lt=Miu Fat Buddhist Monastery|1=Miu Fat Buddhist Monastery}} in {{ill|Hong Kong|en|Hong Kong|qid=Q8646|lt=Hong Kong|1=Hong Kong}}, enshrining [[:en:Sakyamuni|Sakyamuni]] in the centre, {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|qid=Q854773|lt=Bhaisajyaguru|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} on the left and Amitābha on the right]] The proper form of Amitābha's name in {{ill|Sanskrit|en|Sanskrit|qid=Q11059|lt=Sanskrit|1=Sanskrit}} is ''{{IAST|Amitābha|qq=}}'', masculine, and the [[nominative case|nominative]] singular is ''{{IAST|Amitābhaḥ|qq=}}''. This is a compound of the Sanskrit words ''amita'' ("without bound, infinite") and ''ābhā'' ("light, splendor"). Consequently, the name is to be interpreted as "he who possesses light without bound, he whose splendor is infinite". The name ''Amitāyus'' (nominative form ''{{IAST|Amitāyuḥ|qq=}}'') is also used for the [[:en:Sambhogakāya|Sambhogakāya]] aspect of Amitābha, particularly associated with longevity.{{cn|date=January 2021|qq=}} He is mostly depicted sitting and holding in his hands a vessel containing the nectar of immortality. In Tibetan Buddhism, Amitāyus is also one of the three deities of long life (Amitāyus, {{ill|Tara (Buddhism)|en|Tara (Buddhism)|qid=Q746709|lt=White Tara|1=Tara (Buddhism)}} and {{ill|Usnisavijaya|en|Usnisavijaya|qid=Q3552695|lt=Uṣṇīṣavijayā|1=Usnisavijaya}}). ''Amitāyus'' being a compound of ''amita'' ("infinite") and ''āyus'' ("life"), and so means "he whose life is boundless". In Chinese, {{Lang|zh-hant|阿彌陀佛|qq=}}, pronounced "Ēmítuófó", is the Chinese pronunciation for the Sanskrit name of the Amitābha Buddha (Amida Buddha). The "e mi tuo" is the transliteration of the Sanskrit word "amita" which means "boundless" ({{Lang|zh-hant|無量|qq=}}, "wuliang"). "Fo" is the Chinese word for "Buddha".{{cite web|title=Buddhist Charms|url=http://primaltrek.com/bu11hist.html|access-date=22 May 2014|qq=}} In {{ill|Vietnamese language|en|Vietnamese language|qid=Q9199|lt=Vietnamese|1=Vietnamese language}}, {{ill|Korean language|en|Korean language|qid=Q9176|lt=Korean|1=Korean language}}, and Japanese, the same Chinese characters used for Amitābha are used to represent his name, though they are pronounced slightly differently: * Vietnamese: ''A Di Đà Phật'' * Korean: ''Amita Bul'' * Japanese: ''Amida Bosatsu''. In addition to transliteration, the name Amitābha has also been translated into Chinese using {{ill|Chinese characters|en|Chinese characters|qid=Q8201|lt=characters|1=Chinese characters}} which, taken together, convey the meaning "Infinite Light": 無量光 (Wúliàngguāng). In the same fashion, the name Amitāyus ("Infinite Life") has been translated as 無量壽 (Wúliàngshòu). These translated names are not, however, very commonly used. In Japanese, Amitābha is also called {{nihongo|Amida Nyorai|阿弥陀如来||lead=yes|"the [[Tathāgata]] Amitābha"|qq=}}. In Tibetan, Amitābha is called {{bo-textonly|འོད་དཔག་མེད་|qq=}} {{bo|w='od dpag med|s=Öpakmé|qq=}} and in its reflex form as Amitāyus, {{bo-textonly|ཚེ་དཔག་མེད་|qq=}} {{bo|w=tshe dpag med|s=Tsépakmé|qq=}}. They are iconographically distinct. == Iconography == [[File:Amitabha Buddha Statue from Wonju.jpg|thumb|left|Statue of Amitabha from the {{ill|Unified Silla|en|Unified Silla|qid=Q715257|lt=Unified Silla|1=Unified Silla}} period, [[National Museum of Korea|Korea]]. Note the distinct Amitabha {{ill|Mudra|en|Mudra|qid=Q208030|lt=Mudra|1=Mudra}} (symbolic hand gesture).]] [[File:Amitayus Mandala.jpeg|thumb|left|[[Mandala]] of Amitāyus, {{ill|Tibet|en|Tibet|qid=Q17252|lt=Tibet|1=Tibet}}, 19th century, [[:en:Rubin Museum of Art|Rubin Museum of Art]]]] When in the descending standing position, Amitābha is often shown with left arm bare and extended downward with thumb and forefinger touching, with the right hand facing outward also with thumb and forefinger touching. The meaning of this {{ill|Mudra|en|Mudra|qid=Q208030|lt=Mudra|1=Mudra}} is that wisdom (symbolized by the raised hand) is accessible to even the lowest beings, while the outstretched hand shows that Amitābha's compassion is directed at the lowest beings, who cannot save themselves. When not depicted alone, Amitābha is often portrayed with two assistant bodhisattvas, usually {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|en|Avalokiteśvara|qid=Q193849|lt=Avalokiteśvara|1=Avalokiteśvara}} on the right and {{ill|Mahasthamaprapta|en|Mahasthamaprapta|qid=Q1465683|lt=Mahāsthāmaprāpta|1=Mahasthamaprapta}} on the left. This iconography is known as an '''Amitabha triad''', and is especially common in {{ill|Chinese art|en|Chinese art|qid=Q756615|lt=Chinese|1=Chinese art}}, {{ill|Japanese art|en|Japanese art|qid=Q603399|lt=Japanese|1=Japanese art}}, and {{ill|Korean art|en|Korean art|qid=Q929402|lt=Korean art|1=Korean art}}.[https://www.metmuseum.org/art/collection/search/39882 "Amitabha triad"], Metropolitan Museum Amitābha is said to display 84,000 auspicious and distinguishing marks reflecting his many virtues.{{cite book|last1=Olson|first1=Carl|title=The Different Paths of Buddhism: A Narrative-Historical Introduction|date=2005|publisher=Rutgers University Press|location=New Brunswick, NJ|isbn=0813535611|page=[https://archive.org/details/differentpathsof0000olso/page/185 185]|url=https://archive.org/details/differentpathsof0000olso|url-access=registration|access-date=9 June 2016|qq=}} Amitābha can often be distinguished by his {{ill|Mudra|en|Mudra|qid=Q208030|lt=mudrā|1=Mudra}}: Amitābha is often depicted, when shown seated, displaying the meditation mudrā (thumbs touching and fingers together as in the Great Buddha of Kamakura ({{lang|ja|鎌倉大仏|qq=}}) at {{ill|Kōtoku-in|en|Kōtoku-in|qid=Q672056|lt=Kōtoku-in|1=Kōtoku-in}} or the exposition mudrā, while the earth-touching mudrā (right hand pointed downward over the right leg, palm inward) is reserved for a seated Gautama Buddha alone. He can also be seen holding a lotus in his hands while displaying the meditation mudrā. There is a difference between Amitāyus and Amitābha. Amitāyus—the Buddha of Infinite Life—and Amitābha—the Buddha of Infinite Light—are essentially identical, being reflective images of one another. Sutras in which Gautama Buddha expounds the glories of Sukhavati, the Pure Lands, speak of the presiding Buddha sometimes as Amitābha and sometimes as Amitāyus. When depicted as Amitāyus he is depicted in fine clothes and jewels and as Amitābha in simple monk's clothing. They are also simply known as Amida in the Chinese and Japanese tradition. The image of the gold colored statue in the article is of Amitāyus as he is wearing a five-pointed crown, which is the easiest way to distinguish them. Amitāyus is an emanation of Amitābha. Amitābha is the head of the Lotus family.{{cite book|author=Landaw, Jonathan |title=Images of Enlightenment: Tibetan Art in Practice|year=1993|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=i0I1K4tEuVwC|publisher=Snow Lion Publications|isbn=978-1-55939-832-9|pages=75, 80, 96|qq=}} In Vajrayana, Amitābha is the most ancient of the {{ill|Five Tathāgatas|en|Five Tathāgatas|qid=Q265911|lt=Five Tathagatas|1=Five Tathāgatas}}. He is of red color originating from the red seed syllable ''[[:en:Hrīḥ|Hrīḥ]]''. He represents the cosmic element of "Sanjana" (name). His vehicle is the peacock. He exhibits [[Mudra#Dhyāna Mudrā|Samadhi Mudra]] his two palms folded face up, one on top of the other, lying on his lap. The lotus is his sign. When represented on the stupa, he always faces toward west. He is worshiped thinking that one can have salvation. == History and archeology == {{multiple image | perrow = 2 | total_width = 300 | caption_align = center | align = right | direction = vertical | header = Earliest "Amitābha" inscription | image1 = Amitabha Buddha inscription in the year 26 of Huvishka.jpg | image2 = Amitabha Buddha inscription in the year 26 of Huvishka Inscription Buddhasya Amitabhasya.jpg | footer_align = center | footer = Inscribed pedestal with the first known occurrence of the name of "Amitābha Buddha" in "the year 26 of {{ill|Huvishka|en|Huvishka|qid=Q1396420|lt=Huvishka|1=Huvishka}}" (153 CE, first year of Huvishka){{cite book |last1=Rhie |first1=Marylin M. |title=Early Buddhist Art of China and Central Asia, Volume 3: The Western Ch'in in Kansu in the Sixteen Kingdoms Period and Inter-relationships with the Buddhist Art of Gandh?ra |date=2010 |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-18400-8 |page=xxxvii, Fig 6.17a |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ivE2mpfbNR0C&pg=PR37 |language=en|qq=}} In {{ill|Brahmi script|en|Brahmi script|qid=Q185083|lt=Brahmi script|1=Brahmi script}} in the inscription:
[[File:Gupta allahabad bu.jpg|12px]][[File:Gupta ashoka 11h.jpg|10px]][[File:Gupta ashoka sya.svg|19px]] [[File:Gupta ashoka a.svg|19px]][[File:Gupta ashoka mi.jpg|12px]] [[File:Gupta ashoka t.svg|12px]] [[File:Gupta allahabad bh.svg|12px]][[File:Gupta ashoka sya.svg|19px]]
"''Bu-11ha-sya A-mi-tā-bha-sya''"
"Of the Buddha Amitabha"{{cite journal |last1=Schopen |first1=Gregory |title=The Inscription on the Kuṣān Image of Amitābha and the Charakter of the Early Mahāyāna in India |journal=The Journal of the International Association of Buddhist Studies |date=1987 |volume=10 |issue=2 |pages=99–138 |url=http://www.ahandfulofleaves.org/documents/Articles/The-Inscription-on-the-Kusan-Image-of-Amitabha-and-and-the-Character-of-Early-Mahayana-in-India_Schopen.pdf|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191207114137/http://www.ahandfulofleaves.org/documents/Articles/The-Inscription-on-the-Kusan-Image-of-Amitabha-and-and-the-Character-of-Early-Mahayana-in-India_Schopen.pdf|url-status=dead|archive-date=December 7, 2019|qq=}}
{{ill|Art of Mathura|en|Art of Mathura|qid=Q28173968|lt=Art of Mathura|1=Art of Mathura}}, [[:en:Mathura Museum|Mathura Museum]] }} The first known {{ill|Epigraphy|en|Epigraphy|qid=Q181260|lt=epigraphic|1=Epigraphy}} evidence for Amitābha is the bottom part of a statue found in Govindnagar, {{ill|Pakistan|en|Pakistan|qid=Q843|lt=Pakistan|1=Pakistan}} and now located at {{ill|Government Museum, Mathura|en|Government Museum, Mathura|qid=Q6787359|lt=Government Museum, Mathura|1=Government Museum, Mathura}}. The statue is dated to "the 26th year of the reign of {{ill|Huvishka|en|Huvishka|qid=Q1396420|lt={{IAST|Huviṣka}}}]" i.e., 104 CE.Schopen, Gregory. "The Inscription on the Kuṣān Image of Amitābha and the Character of Early Mahāyāna in India". Journal of the International Association of Buddhist Studies, 10 (1987): 99–137 It is a work of {{ill|Kushan art|en|Kushan art|qid=Q85775146|lt=Kushan art|1=Kushan art}}, made during the {{ill|Kushan Empire|en|Kushan Empire|qid=Q25979|lt=Kushan Empire|1=Kushan Empire}} (30–375 CE), and was dedicated to "Amitābha Buddha" by a family of merchants.{{cite web|url=http://old.ykbi.edu.tw/htm/ykbi16/ykbi16_1.pdf|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130612150915/http://old.ykbi.edu.tw/htm/ykbi16/ykbi16_1.pdf|archive-date=2013-06-12 |title=On the origins of Mahayana Buddhism |access-date=2013-06-14|qq=}} {{ill|Gregory Schopen|en|Gregory Schopen|qid=Q3776573|lt=Gregory Schopen|1=Gregory Schopen}} translates the inscription as follows:
The 26th year of the Great King Huveṣka, the 2nd month, the 26th day. On this day by Nāgarakṣita, the (father) of the trader (Sax-caka), the grandson of the merchant Balakatta, the (son of Buddhapila), an image of the Blessed One, the Buddha Amitābha was set up for the worship of all bu11has. Through this root of merit (may) all living things (obtain) the unexcelled knowledge of a bu11ha.
Another early epigraphic mention of Amitabha (c. 610 CE) is found in {{ill|Lalitpur, Nepal|en|Lalitpur, Nepal|qid=Q6647|lt=Patan (Lalitpur)|1=Lalitpur, Nepal}}. It is a verse which states:Lewis, T. T. (1996). [https://college.holycross.edu/faculty/tlewis/PDFs/Sukhavati_Traditions_in_Newar_Buddhism.pdf Sukhavati Traditions in Newar Buddhism.] South Asia Research, 16(1), 1-30. https://doi.org/10.1177/026272809601600101
I praise Amitabha, the best, dispeller of illusion by the light of great {{ill|Prajñā (Buddhism)|en|Prajñā (Buddhism)|qid=Q849649|lt=prajña|1=Prajñā (Buddhism)}}. The light, victor who lives in {{ill|Sukhavati|en|Sukhavati|qid=Q1329565|lt=Sukhavati|1=Sukhavati}} with {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|en|Avalokiteśvara|qid=Q193849|lt=Lokesvara|1=Avalokiteśvara}}, the destroyer of the fear arising in the world, bearer of the lotus, and {{ill|Mahasthamaprapta|en|Mahasthamaprapta|qid=Q1465683|lt=Mahasthamaprapta|1=Mahasthamaprapta}}, the affectionate-hearted one.
Regarding textual evidence, the earliest Buddhist sutra mentioning Amitābha is the translation into Chinese of the ''[[Pratyutpanna Samādhi Sūtra]]'' (般舟三昧經; ''Bozhōu Sānmèi Jīng'') by the {{ill|Kushan Empire|en|Kushan Empire|qid=Q25979|lt=Kushan|1=Kushan Empire}} monk {{ill|Lokaksema (Buddhist monk)|en|Lokaksema (Buddhist monk)|qid=Q370115|lt={{IAST|Lokakṣema}}}] around 180. This text has been dated to between the 1st century BCE and 2nd century CE by modern bu11hologists.{{cite book |title=Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism |date=2013 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=9780691157863 |editor1-last=Buswell |editor1-first=Robert Jr |editor1-link=Robert Buswell Jr. |location=Princeton, NJ |page=674 |editor2-last=Lopez |editor2-first=Donald S. Jr. |editor2-link=Donald S. Lopez, Jr.|qq=}} Other early Mahayana texts mentioning Amitabha include the ''{{ill|Ajitasena Sutra|en|Ajitasena Sutra|qid=Q24906454|lt=Ajitasena Sutra|1=Ajitasena Sutra}}'', ''{{ill|Samadhiraja Sutra|en|Samadhiraja Sutra|qid=Q3632203|lt=Samādhirāja Sūtra|1=Samadhiraja Sutra}}'' and ''[[:en:Shorter Sukhāvatīvyūha Sūtra|Sukhāvatīvyūha Sūtra]].'' The appearance of such literature and sculptural remains at the end of the second century suggests that the teachings on Amitābha we becoming popular in the first and second centuries CE. Furthermore, there are sculptures of Amitabha in dhyani mudras as well as bronzes of Amitābha in abhaya mudra from the {{ill|Gandhara|en|Gandhara|qid=Q213651|lt=Gandhara|1=Gandhara}} era of the first century, suggesting the popularity of Amitābha during that time. One of the last prayer busts of Amitābha can be found in the trademark black stone of the {{ill|Pala Empire|en|Pala Empire|qid=Q668412|lt=Pala Empire|1=Pala Empire}} (c. 750–1161 CE), which was the last Buddhist empire of India. ==See also== * {{ill|Chan Buddhism|en|Chan Buddhism|qid=Q940709|lt=Chan Buddhism|1=Chan Buddhism}} * {{ill|Chinese Buddhism|en|Chinese Buddhism|qid=Q847618|lt=Chinese Buddhism|1=Chinese Buddhism}} * {{ill|Hōnen|en|Hōnen|qid=Q507980|lt=Hōnen|1=Hōnen}} * {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Jōdo Shinshū]]|italic=no|qq=}} * [[Jōdo-shū]] * {{ill|Neo-Confucianism|en|Neo-Confucianism|qid=Q825754|lt=Neo-Confucianism|1=Neo-Confucianism}} * {{ill|Shinran|en|Shinran|qid=Q512934|lt=Shinran|1=Shinran}} * [[Yuzu Nembutsu]] ==Notes== Sutra (Pure Land)] * [http://www.shingon.org/deities/jusanbutsu/amida.html Shingon Buddhism: Amida Nyorai/Buddha of Infinite light and Life] {{Chinese Buddhist Pantheon|qq=}} {{Buddhist Pantheon|qq=}}
